rulings.Case Title: VICKY WOOLLEY, et al VS. ASBESTOS DEFENDANTS (B/P)AS REFLECTED ON EXHIBITS et al
rulings.Rulings: ON ASBESTOS LAW AND MOTION CALENDAR FOR WEDNESDAY, MARCH 16, 2011 IN DEPARTMENT 220 AT 9:30 AM, LINE 1. Defendant Pacific Coast Building Products, Inc.¿s motion for summary judgment is denied. Assuming without deciding that Rich-Tex paid money to the plaintiffs in settlement (so as to avoid an unenforceable sham settlement/assignment) and further assuming without deciding that some of the settlement money that was paid can fairly be allocated to economic damages and/or to one or more chains of distribution of a single asserted defective product (because equitable indemnity only lies for those matters in an asbestos case), the assignment of a equitable indemnity claim to plaintiffs by one asserted jointly liable tortfeasor against another asserted jointly liable tortfeasor is not per se invalid merely because the asserted indemnitor was not sued in the action where the plaintiffs sued the assignor and the settlement/assignment occurred after the statute of limitations would have barred any claim by plaintiffs against the asserted indemnitor. (See generally Valley Circle Estates v. VTN Consolidated, Inc. (1983) 33 Cal. 3d 604; People ex rel. Department of Transportation v. Superior Court (1980) 26 Cal. 3d 744; Fleck v. Bollinger Home Corporation (1997) 54 Cal. App. 4th 926; Bush v. Superior Court (1992) 10 Cal. App. 4th 1374). Defendant did not satisfy its burden of production that plaintiffs do not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence that Douglas Ivance was exposed to an asbestos-containing product supplied by defendant. Plaintiffs¿ interrogatory responses state that Mr. Rayburn observed Mr. Ivance being exposed to asbestos-containing products supplied by defendant yet Mr. Rayburn¿s asserted knowledge is not discussed, much less shown not to exist, in defendant¿s separate statement, thereby precluding defendant from relying solely on Mr. Kasten to satisfy its burden on lack of asbestos content or on plaintiffs¿ written discovery responses to s
